query: efficacy of daridorexant in treating insomnia

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Insomnia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Dual Orexin Receptor Antagonists Suvorexant, lemborexant, and daridorexant block the binding of wake-promoting orexin A and orexin B to OX1 and OX2 receptors, counteracting orexin/hypocretin-mediated nighttime awakening. The FDA does not recommend higher doses of these medications due to safety concerns about the increased risk of next-day driving difficulty, increased daytime somnolence, and narcolepsy-like symptoms, such as hypnogogic-hypnopompic hallucinations, cataplexy, and vivid dreams. These medications are contraindicated in patients with narcolepsy because of possible underlying mechanisms of orexin antagonism.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Insomnia -- Treatment / Management -- Medication classes:
retrieved_docs.content: Gamma-aminobutyric acid-A receptor agonists:  Benzodiazepines and benzodiazepine receptor agonists act on GABA subtype A receptors (GABA A Rs) with sedative, anxiolytic, muscle relaxant, and hypnotic effects. A significant difference between the 2 groups is the receptor affinity towards different subtypes of GABA A Rs. Although all benzodiazepines have a similar affinity for various subtypes of alpha subunits, benzodiazepine receptor agonists have varying affinities for different subtypes of alpha subunits. For example, zolpidem and zaleplon have a higher affinity for the alpha-1 subunit and a lower affinity for the alpha-2 and alpha-3 subunits; however, eszopiclone has a higher affinity for the alpha-2 and alpha-3 subunits of the GABA receptor. The potential advantages of benzodiazepines and benzodiazepine receptor agonists are efficacy for sleep onset and maintenance and a range of half-lives. Potential disadvantages associated with benzodiazepines and benzodiazepine receptor agonists include cognitive and motor impairment, next-day sedation, anterograde amnesia, rebound insomnia, nausea, headaches, complex sleep-related behavior, and long-term risks such as dependence, depression, falls, hip fractures, and dementia. The Beers Criteria recommend avoiding benzodiazepines and benzodiazepine receptor agonists in individuals older than 65. [1] [21] [22] The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has approved 4 benzodiazepines for the treatment of insomnia—temazepam, triazolam, flurazepam, and estazolam. The benzodiazepine receptor agonists approved by the FDA for the treatment of insomnia include eszopiclone, zaleplon, and zolpidem, including extended release and sublingual forms.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Flight Safety Decisions -- Issues of Concern -- Fatigue
retrieved_docs.content: Caffeine is the most commonly used psychoactive drug to promote wakefulness and has been shown to enhance cognitive performance in long-haul aviation conditions. [21] The FAA does not approve the use of prescribed stimulants, or go pills , such as modafinil and dextroamphetamine, for commercial pilots. However, in the military, these medications are used under strict regulations to maintain wakefulness during unavoidable long sorties. Pilots must undergo prior ground testing to assess potential adverse effects before in-flight use. Adverse effects of stimulants include insomnia (the intended adverse effect), decreased appetite, weight loss, hypertension, tachycardia, lightheartedness, headaches, irritability, overconfidence, aggression, and gastrointestinal effects such as diarrhea, constipation, and abdominal pain. There is also limited evidence of increased tics, so caution is advisable for patients with tic disorders such as Tourette syndrome. Technically, modafinil is not classified as a stimulant, but it does have stimulant-like effects. Like stimulants, modafinil does improve alertness and wakefulness. Modafinil has a slower onset with a half-life of 15 hours, so it tends to have fewer adverse effects than dextroamphetamine. [22] Dextroamphetamine has a shorter half-life of 12 hours with a quicker onset, so it tends to have more noticeable adverse effects and requires more frequent re-dosing. Non-pharmacological management of fatigue is considered the first-line approach. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Fatal Familial Insomnia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Discontinuation of medications that may exacerbate confusion, memory, and insomnia is essential.

retrieved_docs.title: Zaleplon -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: This result of insomnia has far-reaching adverse consequences on the physical, social, mental, and emotional health and wellbeing of the patient, including increased risk of accidents, decreased work productivity, increased risk of comorbid psychiatric disorder, decreased quality of life, and increased usage of healthcare resources. Various pharmacologic and non-pharmacologic modalities have been tried to help patients with this condition. Regardless of the chosen modality, therapy aims to improve the quality and quantity of sleep while minimizing daytime impairments as well as improving the overall quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Lemborexant -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Lemborexant is a medication used in the management and treatment of insomnia. It is in the dual orexin antagonist class of medications. This activity outlines the indications, actions, and contraindications for lemborexant as a valuable agent in managing insomnia. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors such as dosing, monitoring, and drug interactions pertinent for interprofessional team members in the care of patients with insomnia and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Lemborexant -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Summarize the management considerations for patients with insomnia. Describe the mechanism of action of lemborexant. Identify the most common adverse effects of lemborexant therapy. Review the importance of collaboration and communication amongst the interprofessional team to enhance the delivery of care for patients receiving lemborexant. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Corticobasal Degeneration -- Treatment / Management -- Symptomatic Management
retrieved_docs.content: Melatonin can manage sleep disturbances like insomnia and rapid eye movement sleep behavior disorder; however, low-dose clonazepam may be necessary if melatonin fails. Given cognitive side effects, other benzodiazepines, benzodiazepine receptor agonists, and tricyclic antidepressants must be avoided. Stimulants, if used for excessive daytime somnolence, must be restricted to low doses.

retrieved_docs.title: Zolpidem -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for zolpidem. Evaluate the mechanism of action of zolpidem. Assess the adverse effects of zolpidem. Implement effective collaboration and communication among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes and treatment efficacy for patients who might benefit from zolpidem therapy for insomnia. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.content: Treatment is largely centered on symptomatic relief and palliative care, as there is no cure for FFI. Different treatment modalities mentioned in the literature are as follows.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Insomnia -- Treatment / Management -- Other Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Benzodiazepines and benzodiazepine receptor agonists can be used for the short-term treatment of insomnia (less than 4 weeks); the advantages and disadvantages of longer-term treatment should be discussed with the patient. Sedating antidepressants at low doses can be used for short-term treatment of insomnia after consideration of contraindications; the advantages and disadvantages of longer-term treatment should be discussed with the patient. Orexin receptor antagonists can be used for the treatment of insomnia for up to 3 months; the advantages and disadvantages of longer-term treatment should be discussed with the patient. Antihistamines, antipsychotics, fast-release melatonin, and herbal remedies lack sufficient evidence to support their use in chronic insomnia and may have potential disadvantages. In patients older than 55, extended-release melatonin may be used for up to 3 months for the treatment of insomnia.

retrieved_docs.title: Short-Term Insomnia -- Treatment / Management -- Additional Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: In older populations, it is essential to consider the Beers Criteria for safe and appropriate prescribing. Medications to avoid include benzodiazepines, antihistamines, and tricyclic antidepressants, as well as other potentially harmful drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: REM Sleep Behavior Disorder(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The primary goal of treatment is to provide patients with a safe sleeping environment for them and their bed partners. Healthcare professionals can achieve this through non-pharmacologic approaches and pharmacotherapy if needed.

retrieved_docs.title: Fatal Familial Insomnia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Several treatments using compounds such as pentosane polysulfate, quinacrine, and amphotericin B have been studied with inconclusive results. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Rapid Eye Movement Sleep Behavior Disorder -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Ramelteon is a melatonin M1/M2 receptor agonist that resembles melatonin FDA FDA-approved for insomnia. [52] Ramelteon at a dose of 8mg at bedtime was studied in an open-label study on individuals with idiopathic RBD. Although there was no significant effect on rates of dream enactment or RSWA, subjective improvement was reported, and no side effects were noted. [53]

retrieved_docs.title: Trazodone -- Indications -- Non-FDA-Approved Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Trazodone is used off-label for patients with insomnia. Trazodone is not FDA-approved for sleep disorders due to insufficient clinical data to justify its use as a sedative agent. [3] Trazodone is also used off-label for anxiety, Alzheimer disease, substance misuse, bulimia, and fibromyalgia due to its serotonergic receptor antagonism and serotonin reuptake-inhibiting effects. [4] Trazodone has also been used for post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) if the first-line treatment of SSRIs does not demonstrate efficacy. The dose of 50 to 200 mg of trazodone has been shown to reduce nightmare episodes of nightmares as well as improve sleep habits in studies involving PTSD patients. According to the American Academy of Sleep Medicine (AASM), trazodone is suggested to treat nightmares associated with PTSD. [5] However, various studies show patients with panic symptoms have suffered exacerbation in some instances, which is why SSRIs, instead of trazodone, are preferred as the first-line treatment for PTSD. Additionally, research has shown trazodone improves apnea and hypopnea episodes in patients with obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), and the drug does not worsen hypoxemic episodes. [6] Trazodone raises the respiratory threshold, lowering the risk of respiratory instability. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1686
retrieved_docs.content: Bromazepam, buspirone, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, prazepam and triazolam are all classified as anxiolytics, a therapeutic group of medications used to alleviate anxiety symptoms. As members of the same drug class, these agents share the primary indication of treating anxiety disorders, although they differ in chemical structure, pharmacokinetics and side‑effect profiles, which influences the choice of a specific anxiolytic for individual patient care. Understanding that each of these drugs belongs to the anxiolytic category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, monitor for class‑related adverse effects, and provide comprehensive management of anxiety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 814
retrieved_docs.content: 7‑nitroindazole, bromazepam, buspirone, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, midazolam, prazepam and triazolam are all classified as anxiolytics. Anxiolytics constitute a therapeutic class of drugs used to reduce anxiety symptoms, and each of these compounds is recognized as belonging to that class. Although they differ in chemical structure and specific clinical applications, they share the common purpose of alleviating anxiety, and selection among them in patient care depends on factors such as efficacy, side‑effect profile, and individual patient needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2760
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308, alpidem, bromazepam, buspiron, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, midazolam and triazolam are all classified as anxiolytics. As members of the anxiolytic drug class, each of these agents is used to alleviate anxiety symptoms and related disorders. Their shared classification reflects common therapeutic goals and, in many cases, similar mechanisms of action that target central nervous system pathways to produce calming effects. Recognizing that these diverse compounds belong to the same pharmacologic group aids clinicians in selecting appropriate anxiety‑reducing treatments and understanding potential cross‑reactivity, side‑effect profiles, and drug‑interaction considerations within this medication class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1558
retrieved_docs.content: Promazine is classified both as an antiemetic and as a dopamine antagonist. As an antiemetic, promazine is used to prevent or treat nausea and vomiting, while its role as a dopamine antagonist means it blocks dopamine receptors, a mechanism that contributes to its anti‑nausea effects and can also be relevant in managing conditions such as psychosis or movement disorders. The overlap between these categories highlights that many dopamine‑blocking agents, including promazine, possess antiemetic properties, which is important for clinicians when selecting appropriate therapy for patients who require control of nausea or benefit from dopamine receptor inhibition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 475
retrieved_docs.content: Imidazole is a heterocyclic chemical scaffold that serves as a parent structure for several therapeutic agents, most notably the antifungal medication clotrimazole, which is an imidazole derivative. Although ondansetron is sometimes mentioned in connection with the imidazole scaffold, it does not belong to the imidazole class; ondansetron is a serotonin 5‑HT₃‑receptor antagonist primarily used as an antiemetic. Antipsychotics constitute a distinct pharmacological class employed to manage psychotic disorders, and they are not a subtype of ondansetron. A well‑recognized adverse effect of long‑term antipsychotic use is “خلل الحركة المتأخر,” the Arabic term for tardive dyskinesia, a late‑onset movement disorder. These relationships underscore the need to differentiate chemical parent structures from therapeutic categories and to remain vigilant for movement‑related side effects in patients receiving antipsychotic therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1333
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308 is classified as an anxiolytic medication. The class of anxiolytics encompasses several specific agents, including bromazepam, buspirone, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, and prazepam. These drugs share the common therapeutic purpose of reducing anxiety symptoms and are employed in the management of various anxiety disorders. Recognizing PD 134308 as part of this pharmacologic group highlights its likely mechanism of action and clinical use alongside the other listed anxiolytic agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2654
retrieved_docs.content: Benperidol, chlorpromazine, levomepromazine maleate, and trifluoperazine hydrochloride are all medications that may be used to treat اضطراب القلق (anxiety disorder). In contrast, mefloquine hydrochloride is contraindicated in patients with اضطراب القلق, indicating that it should be avoided in this condition. Together, these agents illustrate both therapeutic options for managing anxiety disorder and important safety considerations to prevent adverse effects in affected individuals.

Rolapitant is contraindicated with the thioridazine class of phenothiazines. Antimalarial mefloquine and mefloquine hydrochloride are contraindicated in both psychotic and anxiety disorders. Withdrawal phenomena are relevant for psychoactive substances; selective serotonin‑reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) withdrawal is a specific subtype that requires monitoring when discontinuing psychotropic medications. Allergic reactions such as chronic allergic rhinitis are managed symptomatically with diphenhydramine hydrochloride and promethazine hydrochloride, which relieve nasal congestion and itching; diphenhydramine also provides anxiolytic effects for anxiety disorder. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: trifluoperazine hydrochloride, and promethazine hydrochloride—that are used to treat psychotic disorders, anxiety disorders, and nausea, many possessing anti‑emetic activity. Synthetic cannabinoid nabilone and anti‑emetic agents rolapitant, trimethobenzamide hydrochloride, and ondansetron (as an inactive ingredient in oral sodium benzoate solution) also prevent or treat nausea. Antimalarial agents meflochin, mefloquine and mefloquine hydrochloride are contraindicated in patients with psychotic or anxiety disorders. Antihistaminic drugs diphenhydramine hydrochloride and promethazine hydrochloride relieve chronic allergic rhinitis and may address nausea and anxiety; both belong to the phenothiazine structural family. Rolapitant has a class‑contraindicated relationship with thioridazine, indicating that concurrent use is not recommended. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 211
retrieved_docs.content: hydrochloride and promethazine hydrochloride have therapeutic roles in chronic allergic rhinitis (الْتِهابُ الأَنْفِ الأَرَجِيّ الدائم). Safety considerations include contraindications of perfenazin in plasma cell disorders (اضطراب خلايا البلازما), of prochlorperazine edisylate and prochlorperazine maleate when narcotic therapy is present, and of the antimalarial agents meflochin and mefloquine hydrochloride in psychotic disorder. Oxazepam is contraindicated in psychosis. Rolapitant has a class‑level contraindication with thioridazine, precluding co‑administration. The phenothiazine agents require balancing of their antihistaminic, anti‑emetic, antipsychotic and anxiolytic effects. Overall, the “Other antiemetics” class illustrates a complex pharmacologic landscape in which agents share overlapping anti‑emetic, antipsychotic, anxiolytic and antihistaminic properties, and selection of therapy must account for specific contraindications such as plasma cell disorders, concurrent narcotic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: 7‑nitroindazole, PD 134308, alpidem, bromazepam, buspiron, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, midazolam, prazepam and triazolam are all classified as anxiolytic agents. Each of these compounds belongs to the broader class of anxiolytics, which are medications used to alleviate anxiety symptoms. Recognizing that these diverse chemical entities share the same therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate anxiolytic therapy based on individual drug properties, pharmacokinetics, and patient-specific factors, thereby optimizing anxiety management and overall patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1563
retrieved_docs.content: Alpidem, bromazepam, buspiron, chlormezanon, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, meprobamát and prazepam are all classified as anxiolytics. Each of these agents belongs to the broader class of drugs used to alleviate anxiety and related symptoms. Many of them, such as bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam and prazepam, are benzodiazepine derivatives, while others like buspiron and chlormezanon have distinct chemical structures but share the same therapeutic purpose. As members of the anxiolytic category, they are employed in clinical practice to manage anxiety disorders, provide sedation, and, in some cases, treat related conditions such as muscle tension or insomnia, highlighting their interconnected role in the pharmacologic management of anxiety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 299
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiolytics form a therapeutic class used to relieve anxiety and comprise a heterogeneous group of agents including PD 134308, SB 206553, binospirone mesylate, bromazepam, buspirone, chlormezanon, demethyldiazepam, deramciclane, diazepam, estazolam, fluvoxamine, midazolam, prazepam, ritanserin and triazolam. Within this class, diazepam, bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, midazolam, prazepam and triazolam are benzodiazepines that also function as centrally acting muscle relaxants; diazepam and chlormezanon are explicitly classified as centrally acting muscle relaxants in addition to their anxiolytic activity. SB 206553, binospirone mesylate, deramciclane and ritanserin belong to the serotonin‑receptor antagonist subclass, illustrating non‑benzodiazepine mechanisms that block serotonergic receptors, whereas fluvoxamine acts as a selective serotonin‑reuptake inhibitor, linking it to serotonergic modulation but via reuptake inhibition rather than direct receptor antagonism. Important drug‑interaction considerations include contraindications for co‑administration of midazolam with the antiretroviral protease inhibitor saquinavir, and similarly for triazolam with saquinavir, reflecting clinically significant pharmacokinetic interactions likely mediated by cytochrome P450 pathways. Recognizing the shared anxiolytic classification, the distinct pharmacologic actions (benzodiazepine‑mediated GABAergic effects, serotonin receptor antagonism, and serotonin reuptake inhibition), and the specific contraindicated combinations is essential for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1987
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiolytics constitute a therapeutic class that includes several benzodiazepine agents—bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, midazolam, and triazolam—as well as the non‑benzodiazepine anxiolytic buspiron. All of these drugs share the primary function of reducing anxiety symptoms, though they differ in pharmacokinetic profiles, potency, and specific clinical indications. Benzodiazepines such as bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, midazolam, and triazolam act by enhancing the inhibitory effects of gamma‑aminobutyric acid (GABA) at the GABA‑A receptor, leading to rapid anxiolysis, sedation, muscle relaxation, and anticonvulsant effects. Buspiron, in contrast, exerts its anxiolytic action primarily through partial agonism at serotonin 5‑HT1A receptors, offering anxiety relief with a lower risk of sedation and dependence. Recognizing that these diverse agents all belong to the anxiolytic category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy based on the patient’s specific needs, risk of side effects, and potential for dependence.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2786
retrieved_docs.content: Aprepitant, hydroxyzine hydrochloride, and nabilone are pharmacologic agents that can be employed in the management of الغَثَيان. Aprepitant may prevent الغَثَيان, while both hydroxyzine hydrochloride and nabilone may treat الغَثَيان. The use of these medications, each with distinct mechanisms of action, provides clinicians with multiple therapeutic options for preventing or alleviating nausea in patients.
